title: Data Leakage Prevention — High Volume Print to PDF on HR Workstations

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.12 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.12 (Data leakage prevention) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.35: Data Leakage Prevention — High Volume Print to PDF on HR Workstations.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.35' running on WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational EventCode=307 earliest=-7d
| lookup hr_workstation_subnets.csv src_ip OUTPUT vlan
| where vlan="HR"
| stats count as prints by ComputerName, UserName
| where prints>50
description: Monitors print spooler or DLP events for unusual PDF creation volume from HR VLAN.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes windows and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Data Leakage Prevention — High Volume Print to PDF on HR Workstations. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.
implementation: (1) Align with acceptable use policy; (2) correlate with DLP; (3) privacy-safe investigation. Indexes required: `index=windows`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational`; Lookups: `hr_workstation_subnets`, `hr_workstation_subnets.csv`.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Windows Print Service.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Align with acceptable use policy; (2) correlate with DLP; (3) privacy-safe investigation.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=windows sourcetype=WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational EventCode=307 earliest=-7d
| lookup hr_workstation_subnets.csv src_ip OUTPUT vlan
| where vlan="HR"
| stats count as prints by ComputerName, UserName
| where prints>50
```

Understanding this SPL

**Data Leakage Prevention — High Volume Print to PDF on HR Workstations (A.8.12)** — Monitors print spooler or DLP events for unusual PDF creation volume from HR VLAN.

Documented **Data sources**: `WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Windows Print Service. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: windows; **sourcetype**: WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=windows, sourcetype=WinEventLog:Microsoft-Windows-PrintService/Operational, time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where vlan="HR"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by ComputerName, UserName** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where prints>50` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.

Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with the GRC system, audit register, or framework crosswalk. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Bar chart, Table
knownFalsePositives: Incidents may bunch during major events, vulnerability bulletin response, or controlled exercises.
